title: Possessive Articles
description: Learn how to say my, your, his, and her—and how they change disguises depending on the Case!
explanation:
**HOOK**
In English, you just say 'my dog' or 'my cat'. Nice and easy. But in German, your 'my' actually puts on a disguise to perfectly match the gender AND the case of whatever you own!

**CONCEPT**
Possessive articles show ownership: my (mein), your (dein), his (sein), her (ihr). The magical part? They behave EXACTLY like the word 'ein' (a/an).

**RULE**
Start with the base owner word (like 'mein' for 'my'). Then simply glue on the exact same endings you've already learned for 'ein', 'einen', and 'einem'. It is literally just copy-pasting the endings!

tables.title: The Base Owners (Who owns it?)
tables.note: These are the base stems. To use them, you just stick the case endings onto them!

tables.title: How 'mein' changes by Case
tables.note: Look at Akkusativ masculine (meinEN Vater). Look at Dativ masculine (meinEM Vater). This pattern works perfectly for dein, sein, ihr, etc.!

deep_notes.title: WATCH OUT
deep_notes.text:
The word 'ihr' is the ultimate shapeshifter. Beginners panic because 'ihr' can mean 'her', 'their', OR 'you (plural form)'. And 'Ihr' (capitalized) means 'Your (formal)'.
Usually, context makes it obvious! 'Das ist ihr Hund' = That is her/their dog.

exercises.hint: Stift is masculine. 'brauchen' forces Akkusativ. What is the Akkusativ ending for masculine (den/einen)?
exercises.explanation: Akkusativ masculine takes the '-n' ending, so 'mein' becomes 'meinen'!

exercises.question: Ordne die Besitzer (Nom) zu:
exercises.hint: Match the person to their starting possessive word.
exercises.explanation: Great job matching the base possessive articles!

exercises.hint: Hund is masculine. 'mit' forces Dativ. What is the Dativ masculine ending (dem/einem)?
exercises.explanation: mit + masculine Dativ = '-m' ending. unser -> unserem!

exercises.question: Bilde den Satz (I see her brother):
exercises.hint: Bruder = brother (m). sehen forces Akkusativ, so ihr -> ihren.

exercises.question: Fülle die Lücke: 'Das ist ____ Buch (n). (your - informal)'
exercises.hint: Buch is neuter. 'ist' (=) keeps it Nominativ.
exercises.explanation: Neuter Nominativ takes no extra ending! Just 'dein'.

exercises.hint: Kindern is plural Dativ (helfen triggers Dativ). Plural Dativ gets the 'n' party!
exercises.explanation: Plural Dativ always gets an '-n' ending! ihr -> ihren.

exercises.question: Bilde den Satz (That is my bag):
exercises.explanation: Feminine words get the '-e' ending in Nominativ.
